turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Number 53, Alvin R. Campbell, Arnold S. Campbell and Donald Lester, Petitioners, versus United States. Mr. Louison, you may proceed with your argument.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. This case arises out of the so-called Jencks statute, 18 U.S.C. Section 3500, and actually presents what we submit to be a novel question, partially perhaps due to the newness of the statute. The question presented, may it please the Court, is whether or not, after a foundation for the admission of a statement as defined by the statute is laid on cross-examination, is the production of that statement thereby excused when the prosecutor states that the original document is no longer in existence and cannot be produced. And further, that the only statement in the possession of the Government is a report of the FBI agent in charge of the interview which does not meet the definition of the -- of a statement within the statute as laid down by this Court in the Palermo decision. Now, actually, this case arises out of indictments for bank robbery -- for violation of the Federal Bank Robbery Act and resulted in convictions of the three petitioners and maximum sentences of 25 years each were imposed by the District Judge. The decision of the Court of Appeals, actually, does not help us in any respect on the question presented to this Court at this time for the reason that, as shown in the record, the Court of Appeals said that the pretrial report, I refer to page 6 -- 216 of the record, the pretrial report does -- the question concerning the pretrial report does not warrant extended discussion in view of the recent decision of the Supreme Court in Palermo versus United States and that was the subtle substance of what the Court of Appeals in the First Circuit had to say about this point before the Court today.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I didn't get what the page you were reading there.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: 216, Mr. Justice Douglas, of the record and I refer you to the last two lines on that page, in the top of page 217.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The Court of Appeals actually took the principle of Palermo out of context and applied it in its naked existence to a -- an entirely different factual situation and they did that in this manner. In the Palermo case, you had, of course, the situation of a statement which was demanded among others and which did not meet the definition of a statement, as outlined by the statute and ruled on by this Court. In our case, we have the situation where we submit to the Court, there was a statement in existence and that statement was no longer in existence and this report now was the only, if not the best, available evidence of what had formally been the statement of the witness which had been reduced to writing and approved and adopted by him.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: How about the --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: How about the agent himself (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, if Your Honor please, we felt that we did not -- we were not in a position where we should have been required to call the agent as our witness and -- because the witness testified in this case that the agent summary was not precisely as he had stated it. He said, and I think the Government quotes it in their brief and saying there were things in there which were turned around. I refer to page 23 of the Government's brief in which they said they knew that the report contained something different from Staula's testimony since Staula said it was not verbatim. It was turned around and it was not written up just the way the story is, and they refer to their own pages 11 and 12 which sets out excerpts from the record. But if I may -- I didn't want to reach that point at this time but if I may, I will. That imposes upon the petitioners a double burden of proof. Number one, the burden of proof is upon the petitioners to establish that they come within this statute, that so-called Jencks statute. And upon establishing that foundation, as the court referred to it in our present case, upon establishing that foundation, it then becomes incumbent upon the petitioners, if that were so, to show that the Government has no excuse for not producing it and actually, exculpation if the defense would be -- the burden of it would be upon the proponent of the defense or the excused.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is there any suggestion that the destruction was due to any reprehensible or -- or --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- otherwise or -- or --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No, Mr. --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to any extent due to any -- any cause subject to criticism?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No, Mr. Justice. We have expressly stated in our brief and it -- it is our position that that is not material to the issue. We do not make --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that. I --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, let me say this then. There is no evidence in the record thereof. The agent was not inquired of either by the court, by the Government or by counsel for the petitioners who were also trial counsel. They were -- he was not inquired of and as you say you understand, I further make the contention that we do not consider that material. We consider the fact of existence or nonexistence.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that but there's no suggestion that the loss --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: There is no suggestion --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that the loss of view --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- of bad faith or improper conduct on the behalf of any special agent of the Federal Bureau.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- loss of either willfulness or negligence or anything.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, we would have no way of knowing that but we make no suggestion that anything is improper occurred, if that encompasses Your Honor's question.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: That is to say, you mean the instruction or proscribing of the FBI agent's imposed after his reports were prepared, is that what you mean?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes. We make no -- there is no -- there is nothing of record to show how or why or that there was any bad faith involved or reprehensible conduct or misconduct or even negligence. Our position --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, Your --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No, they were not in --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Precisely.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did the agent himself type those -- typed the summary?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The agent himself, Your Honor, as I understand it from what we do have, dictated it and it was prepared by some secretarial help at the Bureau. It was, however, the agent's own summary from what he had received. It does not appear of record whether he was dictating from the statement he took from the witness or whether he was using his own memory. It just appears that he dictated a report to some secretarial or perhaps into a dictating machine which was transcribed. And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where are the references in the record as to what the nature of the so-called original statement was, namely how it was taken, what its volume, on what medium it was expressed, where is all that? Do you have them on the stand?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The witness?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you examine it?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And were there any limits put on the questions you could put therein?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, I -- I can read it for you verbatim so that you will have it now.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Please just tell me where it is.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It's on --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, what witness are you talking about?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- page 200.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You're not --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The -- the witness who gave --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- are you talking about the agent or the witness?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Not the agent, the witness.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm talking about the agent and --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I -- I mis --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was the agent a witness for anybody?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The agent was not a witness for anyone. I -- I think I said before, he was inquired of neither by the court nor the Government nor the petitioners.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understood that to be as to this subject matter but --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry. He was not a -- he was not a witness in any respect nor was any federal agent, except the one who identified photographs of the bank and the automobile involved but there was no --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But would it appear whether he was available?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I would say that it appears that the court said something about the U.S. Attorney would have him available and it was the contention of myself and my colleague at that time that we would not call a special agent as our witness once we complied with the requirements of the statute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not talking about the statute. But generally, was he a witness?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: He was not in court either as a witness or a spectator to the knowledge of counsel.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And he wasn't kept from testifying by the Government so as to lead you to say to the jury, point out the fact that the Government didn't call him.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: We would have no cause to argue the absence of the witness. That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There wasn't any.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He wasn't withheld --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Not such as would --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- give rise to an inference for a jury argument.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct. That's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was he alive? Does one know that, whether he was alive?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, I understand he's an agent in charge of a district office at the present time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But was he -- was it known? Did it appear that he was alive while these proceedings were going on?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It appears, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Not in that many words. No one asked if he were dead or alive. It just --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I know but --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It was merely stated that the agent was available. And as a matter of fact, the United States Attorney said at one -- the judge suggested that he inquired of the U.S. -- of the agent to determine if these statements were in existence when, at that time, the United States Attorney merely announced that he did not have them in his file.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, where in the record is there a description or what -- where can I find a description of the -- of the destroyed document or the non-existing document? The circumstances under which they were given, what the -- the general contents was etcetera, etcetera?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: On page 199 of the printed record, if Your Honor please.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: At -- that's the bottom number.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: There was, apparently, an imperfection in the printing at the bottom number. Page 199, about a little more than a halfway down, it says "Mr. Louison, at this time, we should like to request, under this statute, the statement of this man." The court said, "I am denying it because you have laid no foundation for it as you did the last time. This man said nothing was ever read back to him, so I'm denying it and your rights are saved." At that time, cross-examination was resumed and said, "Now, Mr. Witness, when you --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Who is the witness?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: This is now the -- Staula, the witness whose statement is in question. This is --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: The Government's witness.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The Government's witness, after his direct testimony, yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I don't -- you got me confused. The witness whose statement is in question?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. We've asked for the statement of this witness Staula.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) Alright.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: And he is now under cross-examination by Mr. O'Donnell.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And he gives -- he tells us what he did, what was taken from him and what was --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It says "When you said you had a conversation with the FBI some time less than a week after July 18th, did they write down what you had to say then?" The court, "If you know the witness?" "Yes." And at the top of page 200, "And did they read it back to you, sir?" Answer, "Yes." Cross-question, "And did they ask you if that was essentially what you had just related to them?" "And, did you tell them yes?" And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that all there is in the record on this subject?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, the court then says "I will order it produced. There is a foundation laid for it." Now, when you say is that all there is, Your Honor please, the Government makes note of the fact that, at just below halfway down on the very next page, the witness has been questioned by the court now and -- and he says "If you will --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: By the way, who is the trial judge?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Mr. Justice William T. McCarthy of the Boston Federal Court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Louison, in -- in your colloquy with Mr. Justice Frankfurter, do I understand your answer to be that because of lack of information on your part, you do not know what caused the destruction of this document and you felt that --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It was immaterial to our issue.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- it -- it was not incumbent upon you to -- to call a government agent and make him your witness in order to try to ascertain? It was -- it's your --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- position that that was a responsibility of the Government?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: May I state this, Mr. Chief --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That -- is that -- that --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, precisely, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I should like to state this also in -- in just -- perhaps, paraphrasing a bit. It was our contention that once we met the requirements of the statute, we were entitled to production of the statement. If there were an excuse or a defense so-called for the nonproduction, then it was incumbent or the burden of proof then went to the person who would excuse himself from performance to show the reason for nonperformance and therefore, I felt at the time that I would not, as you stated, call him as my witness --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But it was --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- to ascertain.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The reason for nonproduction, you say, is -- was nonexistent.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- is that in dispute?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That is not in dis -- at the time of trial, we did not know that but--

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand but (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: It is not in dispute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in your case here, is it in dispute that --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That it --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the document to which you were entitled, never mind whether you're entitled to anything, the particular doctrine was not in existence? There's no controversy about that.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's the information furnished to us by the Government and we accept it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And also, that there's no -- and -- and it -- also not in controversy. There's any suggestion that its nonproduction or its nonexistence, if you will, was due to any untoward cause.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I can't say that, Your Honor please, in that very way because all I say is I do not know what happened and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I do not suggest --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- I do not consider it material.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand -- I understand your legal point. I'm trying to get its facts and not as a legal argument which comes up here.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Actually, I have no way of knowing and therefore --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you're not suggesting it.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- I do not suggest it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you're not suggesting it.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: In the absence of knowledge, I do not suggest it, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: When did you find out of its nonexistence? I didn't quite understand.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: We found out that, for the first time, Your Honor please, on appeal. However --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You -- after --after the trial was over?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: After the trial was over.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did you find it out at the trial? Is there anything in the record that showed you knew it during the trial?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: In the trial, Your Honor please, on page --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is that 201 to 202 of the record?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. Actually, the court said, at the bottom of page 201, "Have you got in your possession," addressing the United States Attorney, "any statement that was copied by an FBI agent which, in any way, would reflect a statement that this witness made and which he substantially adopted as the statement?" Mr. Hobley, "No, Your Honor, we do not."

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is he, an assistant to U.S. Attorney?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: He was an assistant to the assistant.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That's -- you're not -- in other words, we have no issue involved here whether you were entitled to a summary. No, I'm sorry, Your Honor.T

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: here is an issue there because I felt that, where there is a new field, there perhaps might be alternative remedies.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, what I'm trying to say, did you add summary?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: You did.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: After they denied you?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes. If I may refer to page 207 of the record, Mr. Justice Brennan, it says, about six straight lines down, just -- well, at the top line, I said, "We move to strike this witness' entire testimony," just below that, I said, "We make a common law demand for that document." At this time, the US Attorney said, "What document?" And I said, "The one he allegedly just read, which was the summary."

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, do you, let me ask you this (Inaudible) that summary has been turned around. Do you take the position that that summary was followed by the statement of the facts?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No, I do not.I just take the position that if the Government removes the situation from the confines or the four corners of the statute, then we go back to the common law and find ourselves back into the Jencks case. May I refer now, Mr. Justice, to page 210 of the record, the -- where it says "Mr. Louison," about a third of the way down, it says "the record is completely without explanation as to the original document."W

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: hat page is that?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: On page 210 of the printed record, about one-third of the way down. Now, if it please the Court, if the petitioners contend that if such a situation as this is permitted to exist and be approved then, actually, we have now found a way for the Government to eliminate all discovery of such documents which, we submit, was neither the intent of the Congress in the passage of this statute nor the intent of this Court, obviously, in the Jencks case and certainly not so in the Palermo case which followed the Jencks case. And we earnestly submit that imposing a double standard -- a double burden of proof upon the petitioners in that regard is equally as bad as the -- giving the Government the power to avoid discovery by destroying the original document or -- when I say original document, I mean the document that would meet the definition of the word "statement" within the statute. In that regard, I might say that, though the witness said he thought he had signed the statement, there is actually no requirement in the statute that he had -- that he sign the statement. This is one form of adoption of it but if he read or had it read to him and approved and adopted it, then it is certainly a statement within Section 3500 just as much as if would have signed it. And therefore, the fact that it does not appear, let's say, conclusively in the record that it was signed by the witness does not preclude us from obtaining it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you talking about the nonexistence of it?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Not -- not the one that's referred to by the judge. Have you got -- on page 201, have you got in your possession any statement that was copied by an FBI agent? They were talking about the -- the document which you asked for which was in existence and the production was denied, is that right?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, but I would say this, Your Honor, in addition.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, before you -- is there any -- does the Government controvert your position that the document taken from the witness, the original document, the document that they are talking about?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Comes within the statute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Comes within the statute?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: They do in one spot and I refer you to page 20 --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I see --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- 26 of the Government's brief in a footnote in which they say that the final testimony of the witness was that he thought it was read back to him. But if you notice, the witness also said, "I think they wrote down what I said and I think they gave it back to me to read over to make sure that it was right and I think had to sign it. Now, I am not sure. I couldn't remember before." Well, that does not actually show the entire nature of the record because, on page 201 of the record from which this excerpt was taken, the very next sentence by the court is -- that's about all the way down the bottom, about six or eight lines up. The court said, "And he said it was read back to him. So, if that," referring to the FBI summary, "if that is a transcript." Now -- actually I can actually remember this witness as if it were yesterday. He was saying "I think they wrote it down and I think they read it back to me to make sure that it was correct and right," but he was actually stating a fact. I would say it's uncontraverted that he gave a statement to the FBI which they wrote down.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As I --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: And the Government's footnote on page 26 of their brief does not reflect the true --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, as I read --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- nature of the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- as I read their brief, and that's all I've done, they do not argue that the original statement was outside of the Jencks case because if that was true, it would be a fortiori to the non-accurate or fair subsequent summary of it was not within -- I mean the whole brief, presupposing that the original thing, which is nonexistent, could have been proffered and should have been produced had they been in existence.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, that's probably so. That's our contention.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I assume your argument can go out on that basis.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I don't -- I'm afraid I don't understand, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Our -- my -- my argument can continue on the basis that it should have been produced.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand the case, the Government's case to assume that the original nonexistent things could have been called for because if they could not have been called for, a fortiori --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that the--

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- unfair summary of it happened.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Certainly. Now, the Government has also said by way of excusing the situation, and they seem to rely on various actual situations in an attempt to excuse it, that the trial judge saw the document in camera and did not see fit to cross-examine the witness on the basis of it and he, being the impartial master of the trial, could have done so if he were disturbed by any apparent or alleged inconsistency. And the short answer to that, I think, was spelled out in the Rosenberg case where it said that it's within the realm of counsel's discretion to determine whether or not there would be any useful matter contained in the -- in the statement for cross-examination and it would not be the province of the court to second-guess counsel in that regard. And of course, they state that -- I want to reach this point rather quickly, they state that if error appears in this trial, it pertains only to the petitioner Lester because he was the one who was named by this witness or identified by this witness. Now, I say this. Where the original document, assuming that it should have been produced if it had been in existence, where the original document is no longer in existence and cannot and has not been preserved for review by any appellate court, we are unable to determine whether or not there is any prejudice to any of the other petitioners. And therefore, it must be held to be prejudicial to all if it is prejudicial to any, and this occurs because of the fact that -- this occurs because of the fact that a statement might contain things that the witness did not testify to in his examination which might be the subject of cross-examination as to "why didn't you say this, Mr. Witness, when back, so close to the time of the event, you did say this?" And of course, the statute there, again, does not say that the statement to be producible must relate only to what the witness stated on his direct examination. It states that it must be related to the subject matter and therefore, matters contained in the statement to which he did not testify to, provided they related to the subject matter, would be the proper subject of production and subject to cross-examination. So -- and of course, there is also the position in the Jencks case that inconsistency is not a prerequisite to production and of course, the statute does not make any such requirement that there'd be a preliminary showing of inconsistency. So, without the necessity of showing inconsistency, as well as the situation where the nonexistent document cannot tell you -- you, Members of the Court, what was contained there in its original form. The prejudice, if there would be any, is to all the petitioners and not to any one particular petitioner because this man merely stated his name on the direct testimony. Now, there is one other point which I think is essential and that is the Government has stated that when must they have it in their possession if it is called for, and they say it must be in their possession at the time production is demanded. And I submit to the Court that that is untenable and we have included in our brief reasons showing that it must be held to be at any time. If the statement were in existence and in the possession of the United States at any time, then it is -- comes under the purview of the statute and the petitioners are entitled to protection from it, from the destruction thereof at any time after.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Let's see if I -- if I understand your argument. I thought, at least it seemed to me clear, that your argument could be -- proceed on the assumption that if the nonexistent documents were in existence, they -- a call for them would have -- would have had -- would have had to be honored.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I also understood you to answer quite clearly to Justice Brennan that you do not think the existing document which was denied to you does not come as such within the Jencks statute --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and could not be called for under the Jencks statute.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Therefore, I do not see why you recur to what the statute requires because the statute conceded, as I get the Government's faith, would have required the production of the nonexistent document and the statute concededly also by you as such would not require the existing document. And your claim is because the existing -- to be required document no longer exist, you have a right to call for the other document.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Well, we have a right to obtain sanctions. My -- my colleague is now going to address the Court on the question of striking out the motion to strike.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But if all (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: We are entitled to sanctions.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But they derive from two statements that I -- that I put to you to find out whether you agree or not, namely the destroyed document concededly could have been called -- the nonexistent document concededly could have been called for and the existing documents concededly is not callable under the statute.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: In the first instance, that's so.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, that was (Inaudible) not entitled to have all with the exception of restrictions under (Inaudible) Alternatively, at least you are entitled to have, under the Jencks case (Inaudible) the Government did have even though it does not qualify.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That is exactly so, Mr. Justice Brennan, and I think Mr. Justice Frankfurter put the question to me that "outside of this situation, would you concede that this statement did not come within the statute as such?" And I said yes, but I do not think that that reflects on the answer I just gave you.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was the statement ever made available to you?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: At no time, Your Honor, except during the appellate matter before the Court of Appeals. It was impounded and that's --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- a matter of record. It was impounded and it was opened for the first time in the Court of Appeals and was never available to petitioners at any time during the trial of this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But what --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Up to this time -- but at -- you did learn the contents of at the time of the --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: At the time of the hearing for the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What was the reason for that?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: The Court of Appeals clerk's office just told me the Court of Appeals was not bound by an impoundment order of the District Court and they opened it just like that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, did you -- did you, on the basis of that knowledge, make any move either before the Court of Appeals in the light of knowledge to show that real prejudice would -- would result from -- or did result from your -- from the failure to make that available at the time?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is that? I don't mean in argument. I mean, did you make a motion saying you now know there are things of this non-disclosed document which are important to bring before a jury?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: In our matter of -- in the -- one of our points in the appeal to the Court of Appeals, we claimed it was error and showed that inferences to be taken from that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand you --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- could have resulted in cross-examination.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- I understand you said it was error but did you affirmatively say, on the knowledge would you -- which was given to you in the Court of Appeals, you are now in the position to show either that the Court of Appeals or the new trial should be had or that the case should go back to the District Court --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: If your --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and there, you could move.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: If your question means, Your Honor, did we do something in addition to the perfection of our appeal, the answer is no.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: We continued with our appeal and that being one of the points of our appeal.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but when you appealed at the time -- at the time you appealed, when you perfected your appeal, you did not know the contents of the document, did you?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But afterwards, you did know the contents of the document and my question is whether, on the basis of that knowledge, having been put in possession of a content, it might demonstratively show that you suffered for not having been allowed to produce that document before a jury. You should now on attack and not on mere legal argument be allowed to go before a jury.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No, the court had in camera inspected that document and had full knowledge of it and we had no reason to believe he would make any different decision on a motion than he had originally upon our demand both for the document to strike the testimony and for production of the summary.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you couldn't ignore what the Court of Appeals with Judge Magruder and Judge Hartigan and Judge -- who was the third? Woodbury would have done, could you?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Obviously, from the very short nature of their opinion on this point, they didn't do very much.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm saying that you couldn't have known what they would have done if you said --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I couldn't have anticipated --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: "Having now this knowledge --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- their actions, no, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- I ask that I be given an opportunity to present this grievance wrong to my client to the jury," could you?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's right. That's a matter of argument.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it's the fact that you didn't do it, isn't it?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's correct. In addition to the appeal, no.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Connor.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Mr. O'Donnell.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Mr. O'Donnell for the petitioner, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, oh, I beg your pardon. Mr. O'Donnell, yes.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. May it please the Court. I would like to devote my attention to the following question. If the Government fails to produce a prior written statement of a government witness who has testified, which statement qualifies under the Jencks Act because of a government agent's destruction of said statement, ought the witness' testimony be stricken from the record. This, in substance, is the question raised by parts one and two of the petitioner's brief. The Court of Appeals did not face this question but rather, dismissed the petitioner's contention perfunctorily by citing the Palermo decision which, of course, dealt with a question totally unrelated to the question I have just posed. Section (d) states that if the U.S. elects not to comply with a not up or a producible statement under (b) and (c), then the court shall strike the testimony of the witness from the record. If we look at Sections (b) and (c), and also (a), we say that they refer to statements in the possession of the United States. The Government says that this necessarily means in the possession of the United States at the time production is called on and, in addition, claims that this position is self-evident. The petitioners do not claim the halo of self-evidence for the Government's position.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In your position -- your position would be the same, would it not, if there had been no other statement?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I -- I would like to say this, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, that, of course, this entire notion of destruction -- alleged destruction which appeal -- appeared for the first time in the Government's brief in opposition to our petition for certiorari and representing these petitioners, I don't embrace the notion of destruction. I think the entire lack of production of the statement runs in with -- I would make the suggestion that they were attempting to insulate this witness and that the trouble and difficulty surrounding getting at this statement was the obvious fact that it had impeachment material in it. Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you're not going to announce this --

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: But --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you now implying that you're agreeing with your associate that there was something skullduggery about the Government's action?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Well, I -- I'm not disagreeing with my associate but at the same time, I'm not reluctant to say that there may very well have been skullduggery. I think there's a fair inference from the record that such tactics were employed.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that -- you're disagreeing with your associate.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: It might be, may it please the Court.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: I don't --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But it's a very common thing for -- it's a very common thing for people -- when people write out a statement to -- to destroy the underlying document. Mr. Secretary gives his whole diary based on that procedure.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Well, may I say this, Mr. Justice --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that a legal document?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: -- Mr. Justice Frankfurter, that this particular agency, the FBI and the little expression that has crept into this case, the word "knows" as though someone was scribbling in a dress or a shopping list on the back of an envelope or a telephone number, this particular agency houses all sorts of records, millions, I would bet, of fingerprints. And I would say that a well-paid FBI agent, competent, out investigating a bank robbery, interviewing an allegedly eyewitness of said bank robbery wouldn't long be tolerated if he didn't take careful notes of what had happened. And I might add that when the summary of the Interview Report which has been stated doesn't come within the purview of the Act, I had objected to the fact when the judge took that summary in camera that in order to make his judgment, he decided he should show it to the witness who had just testified on direct examination, may it please the Court, and also was placed under cross-examination. So, the very obvious thing to me, as trial counsel in that courtroom, was "Gosh! This fellow is going to notice the minute he looks at this that it's not consonant what he testified to on direct." I implore the Court to look at the summary of FBI Agent Toomey. Dominic Staula went in that courtroom and identified three defendants, positive and partial. The summary of Toomey, the FBI agent, he said, "I never saw a third person in the bank." He gave one description and there was clearly impeachment material in that summary. It --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Could I go back to your basic position which, as I understand it, is that once -- once you've laid a foundation by showing that there was a statement then, as a matter of law, the Government doesn't produce it, you are entitled to have his testimony stricken. That's your basic position.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Harlan.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Now, supposing the Government had said, "Alright, we haven't got this statement. We will now produce the witness to whom this statement was made and we'll put him on the stand and examine him ourselves to show what this statement was." Under your view, could you have excluded that testimony and stood on your rights to have the statements -- to have the witness' testimony stricken?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You could've?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. O'Donnell, I'd like to say this. I don't know a thing about it but I'd like to know on the basis of what facts I'm to decide this case.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's one thing for me in a very serious case to suggest or to -- to proffer to this Court, to submit to this Court a case even on fragile testimony of skullduggery by the prosecution. No one feels most strongly about that, no one could feel most strongly about that than I do. It's a very different thing to have it disavowed and then to have co-counsel trot it out again.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: No, may it please the Court, Mr. Justice Frankfurter. It wasn't so much that I trotted it out. I put it on the basis of whether or not I, as a rational being, could take that inference from the entire flavor of what's on this record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As a lawyer before this Court, you have to take a position not as a -- what the flavor is and as a rational being, you either take the position or you don't take the position.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: I've taken a position, may it please --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And your colleague took one position, you take another. You're free to do so because you represent, I take it, a different client.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: No. May I say this, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, that when the premise you established for a judgment exercised asking "would you consider that there might have been good faith or bad faith?" Are now -- I would say, and my argument develops, that it's (Inaudible) of no consequence whether it was good faith, bad faith, indifference, whim, caprice --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is still a different que -- alright, that's a question of law. I understand that quite clearly. But whether you do or do not charge the Government with wrongdoing is, to me, vital in this case.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Alright, may it please the Court. I know --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I can well understand the legal proposition doesn't make any difference. You won't discuss it, but you did discuss it and you did make the charge.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. May it --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. O'Donnell, as I understood your -- your associate, I -- I may be entirely mistaken about it. As I -- as I understood him, he didn't vouch for the fact that there was -- there was nothing wrong with what the Government did. All he said in response certainly to my question was that for want of information, for want of knowledge on the subject, as disclosed in this record, he made no charge of -- of wrongdoing.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: That's precisely my point.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, if -- if he said more than that, I -- I didn't correctly understand him but that's all I understood him to say.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He explicitly disavowed wrongdoing by the Government in an answer to a question by me. He explicitly disavowed it.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: May I say this, Mr. Justice Frankfurter. In this case, where the Government has come here before the United States Supreme Court and introduced a transcript that wasn't before the Circuit Court of Appeals and wasn't in the trial court, I'll take this position. We filed affidavit of -- affidavits of bias in this case. I can't just -- I don't like to raise and the -- we asked for severance in this case as against including these three defendants. It was denied. Now, the Government in its brief is talking about, well, if there's reversible error, it might apply to one and not to the other. Well, I say that's a late date and the -- going further on the concurring opinion of the Palermo case, it was said Congress made crystal clear its purpose only to check extravagant interpretations of Jencks while reaffirming the basic holding that the defendant in trial be entitled to statements helpful in cross-examination of government witnesses who testify against him. Petitioner submit that "entitled" means to have a right to and if the petitioners have a right, then the Government has a corresponding obligation to comply with that right. If there is no obligation, there is no right. Now, if this obligation can be abrogated by the mere act of an investigative agent destroying a statement, even if in good faith, then the right is, in reality, no right at all. If the principles set forth in the Jencks case are subject to whims, caprice or even negligence and carelessness of government agents, then the spirit of Jencks is dead and we are left with the clops.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: But you did have an opportunity and means of reconstructing the statement --

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: May it --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- by calling a witness.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: The FBI agent?

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Now, I say --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You say the Government has got to suffer the consequences of failing to produce which, by hypothesis, let's presume for a moment, was a perfectly honest failure to produce. Take it on that premise at least. And you say the Government has got to suffer the consequences of its failure to produce without your laying any further foundation by trying to resort to the testimony of the agent to reconstruct the statement that's not in existence.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Harlan. Now --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is it true -- does the Act -- a conference in chambers while Mr. Staula was on the stand, counsel for the Government advised you that there had never been a different statement by the witness at all, as cited in Footnote 3 of the Government's brief on page 10?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: That there had never been --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: There's never been a -- that -- that counsel for the Government states several times that the original note of the FBI agent were no longer in existence and that there never had been a different statement by this witness, citing transcript one statement 05.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is that the right statement?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Mr. Justice, yes. We don't contend that there had ever been a -- a written statement by witness Staula but we do contend that we laid a foundation for a producible statement under the various facilities set out in the Jencks Act.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, if there wasn't a written statement, how can you get a written statement that doesn't exist? I don't understand.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: It was written by the agent and adopted by the -- the government witness, Dominic Staula.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You mean the statement that was unavailable? Is that the one you're taking about?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Harlan.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Not the other case?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: No, we've never made --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Brennan. The Government contends, as it's been brought up, since the petitioners did not attempt to call the agent who took the statement, there was no reason to strike the testimony. This is the most tenuous of the Government's contentions. Petitioners had a right to the statement signed and adopted by the witness and to say that the testimony of the agent was a sufficient surrogate is to ask this Court to take up precedence in the fragile house of mechanical jurisprudence. To attempt to equate the written statement of the witness, given a short time after the robbery with the memory of a government agent months later, is to engage in a flagrantly vacuous pleading and it's to proceed as if the Jencks decision had never been handed down and as if the Jencks statute never became law.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: This witness may have had a vivid memory. Maybe he could have given it to you verbatim. How do we know?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice Harlan, that -- the particular witness that we're talking about, Staula, on the stand, made a statement in answer to the trial court that this caption "FBI Agent Interview Report," he said it was inaccurate --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: -- from this particular witness.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: He said it wasn't that a -- if that's the true copy of what he said, isn't that what he did?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, there --

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: They -- he said there were things turned around, may it please the Court.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: How do you explain the testimony of record 201 and 202 after your colloquy there? Witness Staula said that (Inaudible) and I'm trying to rack my brain to think about what happened. I think they wrote down what I said, and then, I think they gave it back to me to read over to make sure that it was right and I think had to sign it. Now, I am not sure. I couldn't remember before," dash and so forth. Now, he, thereby, expressed some uncertainty after that colloquy when he were called up to the bench and on his counsel, isn't that what happened?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: I would qualify that, Mr. Justice Whittaker, as that colloquy indicates that he had doubts as to his signature, merely his signature. Previously, on three occasions, he went into the foundation. I can recall when asking them the questions to establish a foundation, I held in my hand the public document setting out the Jencks Act in an attempt to relate the questions that would satisfy the demand for producible statement.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Am I right in -- in saying that the crux of your argument will be that your argument reducibly fell to this. That if a man makes a statement or if they comes into existing statement, which is producible under the Jencks statute and that statement is lost for the most honest of reasons, the person whose statement it is cannot be utilized as a witness.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It must be of acquiescence, doesn't it?

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, but I'd say, in this case, may it please the Court, in the Government brief, they cite a -- a relevant hypothesis. They talk about a statement being burned but we're not dealing with an act of God in this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it doesn't make -- if it's-- if, according to your argument, I see no difference how the non-availability of the document is brought about, whether through carelessness, except -- except purposeful design to defeat the ends of the law. And that's what my question was.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If the document is -- ceases to be, for the most unassailable reason, the witness -- the person whose -- with reference to whom the statement is made that is no longer producible cannot be availed of the witness by the adverse party.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's the case.

turns.name: LAWRENCE F. O'DONNELL
turns.text: And I would say that the Government adopts the petitioners' contentions. Of course, it would just notice on government agencies that they shall take care, especially when we think of the multitudeness record year in and year out that are maintained by the Government, that it will maintain these records in accordance with the war of the land and have them producible at a later trial. Now, the Government also contends that when I spoke about the act of God, we're, of course, on the fire. We're talking about the act of an agent and also, with that thought in mind, the protection of the Government, it seems that this statute clearly indicates that in the event of a producible statement not being produced, that the testimony of the witness will be stricken. And I say that, in summation, that the petitioner submit that the failure of the Government to produce the statement of the witness in compliance with the order of the court because of the destruction of that statement by a government agent, is the equivalent of an election not to produce and hence, the testimony of the witness, Staula, ought to have been stricken from the record. To hold otherwise would be to stultify the decision of this Court handed down in the Jencks case and to proclaim the inability of the Jencks Act to carry out the legislative intent.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Connor.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Mr. Louison, Mr. O'Donnell, and may it please the Court. The Government distinctly takes the position that no statement has been adequately shown to exist on the record that is before us nor was it in the court below. And --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: The statement in the statutory sense.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: In the statutory sense, that no statement that's alleged to have been destroyed here or was ever shown to exist sufficiently to require a reversal here or to bring into play the doctrine which has been asserted by counsel for petitioner.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I wonder if you tell the Court that since Palermo, there have been any administrative internal rules or regulations or change of practice for the destruction of the original notes and putting them into summary for so long?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Your Honor, I have consulted with the officials of the FBI in preparation for this argument. I also know of my own knowledge from the past practices and I would like to say this. That since the Jencks decision, there has been no changes in FBI procedures. Procedures were changed somewhat in June, just after the Jencks decision. Although, even there, there was not a great alteration of technique and since then, they haven't been changed at all in this respect.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Is there any change made after Palermo?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor. And the changes that were made after Jencks were all calculated to aid the Government in complying with the Jencks decision and later, with the statute. We didn't even change our procedures after the statute was enacted because it wasn't necessary. The --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, the term "raw files," as used by the Department, I'm sure, referred to a typewritten matter which is put in the files and which may contain all sorts of things of a hearsay character describing investigative techniques. It may contain scandal, gossip, any number of things and it may contain a lot of matter which is not segregated by subject classification. For example, in a case, there might be something said about the subject's personal life and on the same page, there might be something said which relates to the criminal conduct for which he is eventually charged and --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think there -- there's been a misconception of just what did happen there or what Staula's testimony on this point is and our brief develops it. In the opening part of our brief, we start on page 4 to describe the testimony of Staula on this point and it will be seen that, at one point, Staula said that -- first of all, he told his story and then the man asked him a few questions, that is the FBI man and then he -- he wasn't too sure whether he signed it. And on page 9 of our brief, we quote from the record --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was he sure that he'd been -- it had been shown to him and that he had approved it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no. He -- I think he's highly equivocal on this point. He thought, at one stage, that he had signed something and there is one portion of the record where he says that when he went down to the police station the day after the robbery, he thought he signed something saying he was in the bank. "But I didn't sign any statement," he said or he didn't sign any lengthy document. Now, on page 8 and 9 of our brief, we've set out one portion of the testimony on this. Down at the bottom of page 8, we've quoted. The court, "Now, Mr. Witness, you testified here on cross-examination that you talked to the FBI agent and that he asked you questions, as I remember it, and that you didn't know what he had written down because it was not read back to you. Didn't you testify that way?" The witness, "I didn't remember. Since then, I have recollected. He didn't actually ask me questions. I mean, at first, I told him the story and then, when I got through, he asked me a few questions." Now, at this point, the court cuts him off by saying, the court, "Well, did he read it back to you?" The witness, "I believe he did." The court, "What is your best memory of it?" The witness, "I am pretty sure he did." Well, now, if I could stop there for a moment, it's customary in FBI interviews to read back to -- to a person from the notes that the agent has taken to confirm and verify the facts that the agent understands he's just been told, so there won't be any mistake about it. That doesn't mean that the agent, at that time, has in his hand a complete elaborate written statement. The agent's notes may be nothing more than hieroglyphics or personal shorthand, which they frequently are, and I think that this is what is involved here. I think this is --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But whether it's --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- the best inference to be drawn.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- whether it's hieroglyphics or -- or whether it's in English, if -- if it is recorded, it might be shorthand. But if it is recorded and it is acknowledged by the witness to have been what he stated, is it not -- does it not then come under the Jencks Act?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, because these notes have never been considered to be a statement in the sense of a -- a written statement signed by the witness nor are they a recording of -- are they a verbatim transcription of any kind or a verbatim statement and they have never been thought by us to have any value as -- as a producible statement under the Jencks Act.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Why does the agent asked him then if that's what he stated?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Because in the process of interviewing a witness, the agent wants to make sure that he understands the story correctly and he may confirm certain points with him. Now, he may not necessarily read back to him what's in his notes. He may just clarify a few points. Maybe his notes will be more elaborate than this. Maybe they will be less so. This, we don't know from the state of the record here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's difficult, Mr. Connor. Am I wrong in -- in having inferred from your -- what you regard as the -- the correct question before the Court that the -- the assumption of that question, presupposition is that the Government assumed that if the notes had been in existence that Staula gave, they were produced. They would have been produced.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor. We do not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Am I wrong about that?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: We -- we do not agree with that presupposition.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I don't understand your questioning. I must say, it doesn't mean -- it isn't -- if I can read English, to say the question is whether the notes of an interview which the witness testified had been read back to him and might have been signed by him were not in existence at the time they filed, the Court should have directed that they'll be turned over to defend for later report by the government agent which the witness testified was not a substantially verbatim account. Now, on the context of the subject matter, there is a differentiation, just the position between notes that have been read back to him, which might have been signed by him and a document, a later document, not being a substantially verbatim account of what he had told. I don't see why the Government doesn't start out with the first point then. Number one, the notes originally were not producible because they were not subject to the Jencks statute.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, that is our position, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that isn't what you say anywhere.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, in our --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- in our brief, we have a counterstatement of the question involved and perhaps, I was direly tearing up beginning with that because we do not agree that a complete foundation for it is made or was made in this case. That is where we disagree. On page 2 of our brief, we formulate a counterstatement, namely that whether --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what I read it for --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that's what I read it from.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: But -- but we -- we differ on this.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that means to me, the -- that -- the implication is clear that if the notes that were not in existence had been in existence, they were not challenged to be non-producible. I must -- I must say it's very strange English.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I understood you to answer with me that the notes were in existence but the summary was the only one that was at the trial.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no, Your Honor. From all appearances, the notes were not in existence. This is what the Assistant U.S. Attorney informed the court of -- below.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: He said he didn't have them but that --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, and he doesn't say affirmatively, the Assistant doesn't, in the record that he talked to the agent but we assumed that he made some inquiry and this is what he understood to be the case. The notes were undoubtedly destroyed because it's standard FBI procedure to do so, unless they have evidentiary value. If the notes had -- have been signed by a person or if the notes contain information --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, then --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- which can be --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- if the notes are always destroyed, that was what my original question was aimed at then --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- Palermo gives us an around -- a way around the entire statute.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no, Your Honor. The destruction of notes is in no way calculated to --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I'm not saying in purpose.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- defeat the statute.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I mean, I don't say that's the purpose of the destruction of notes but if that is the fact, if that is the system, then the -- the statute is -- has no force in the fact.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that -- that is so only on the assumption that the notes always come within the scope of the Palermo -- of the statute.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is right, Your Honor --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, the summary --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- but that is --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: It's clear from Palermo that the summary never does come within.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, but the FBI has not stopped taken written statements where it's appropriate. Now, how they determine this hinges on -- on the -- the reality's investigative light and -- and what the agent sees at the moment. It also hinges on whether the witness is willing to give a statement on how crucial his testimony is likely to become in an -- a number of other things. In this case, at the time that they interviewed Staula, it was the day after the robbery and they still didn't even know who the -- the suspects were. They were looking for general information about the robbery. And on this point, it's important to point out that Staula, this same day after he talked to the FBI agent, was taken by the city police down to a service station where he looked at an individual and he said he couldn't positively identify that man. He then, in the police car, was shown a number of photographs and out of these photos, he picked Lester as being the man who told him to get back against the wall in the bank. And that is how Staula perfected his identification of Lester. The FBI man never talked to Staula again. This -- this is -- is, I think, a partial explanation of what happened here. When the FBI man talked to Staula, he was interested in the general picture and we don't know what kinds of notes the FBI man took. But whatever he took, he wrote up this interview, which is in the record, this Interview Report which is in the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, of course, the word "notes" and the conception of notes is itself a very ambiguous term --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- from the record that has come before this Court entirely here. There are any number of instances where the FBI takes a coherent consecutive statement and the man is trying to draw an issue of it. I wouldn't call those notes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, and we don't call them notes, Your Honor. We call that a written statement. The FBI is very careful that when it gets a statement, it is an ample one and there is very little doubt about it because it -- it always contains a prelude about the individual being advised of his rights and it ends up with who the agents were and the date of the statement. The statement usually covers the subject involved quite -- quite amply and it's something that's almost instantly recognizable. Now, the other kind of report, the report of an interview of a witness, runs somewhat in the form of the one that's in the record here. And this is a very usual procedure because the agents, in the course of the day in having to interview many witnesses or here, trying to discover who did this robbery, may not have time to get a complete written statement from every person that they talked to and in this case, they apparently didn't. They were, apparently, scores of witnesses to this robbery who testified at the trial. Staula was just one of many.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, they're not only -- these notes often are also interluded with comment by the agent. How (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, indeed.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They -- they make comments that this fellow, "I don't believe him" and so on, all sorts of things.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Oh, the agent might note that the witness is nervous or any number of things like that. This would hardly be a statement of the person and under the Jencks concept. It would be unfair to confront the witness with this when he was called to the stand to try to impeach him with this kind of material.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, he'd hardly -- hardly show the witness that kind of notes and ask him to sign up though, would he?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, he wouldn't and I don't know of any FBI practice of a witness signing notes. I -- I really don't, Your Honor. They have no -- no established procedure of -- of having witnesses sign notes.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, that was just Staula.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do they have any --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Except where -- where a witness might draw a sketch or something like that. They might have him sign it in that circumstance. Excuse me, Your Honor?

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: So if Staula's recollection that he did sign this is correct, then it would follow that these things were more than just simply notes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct. And under established procedures, clear procedures, if he had signed those notes, they would be retained. That is, unless the agent violated his duty here, those notes would have been retained and kept in an envelope in the case file.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, then --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- then we have a question of whether it is a statement adopted by the witness.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, I certainly do, Your Honor because the notes may be very incomplete. They may not be --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well -- well, my -- my reason for saying that I don't think it qualifies is that these notes could be very incomplete --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I'm talking about the summary.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: You mean the -- the inter --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- the report of interview here.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, if he had read that over and approved it --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think that, certainly, that would come in. It would certainly be allowable. We have no argument on that.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, it's not the important thing if a complete statement were made up at the time of interview and approved by the witness.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is wanted is the authentication.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But this was your witness who said that to take your -- your own view of the testimony as most favorable of the Government, that he thought it was handed to him. He thought he read it. He thought he signed it. Now, that's your -- that's -- that's your witness and as I understand it, there's nothing contrary to that in the record, is there, and -- and why shouldn't you be bound by it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor. He -- he said, at one point, "I didn't sign anything. I just -- and then there was a cross-question. "Alright, you didn't sign it." Then later, he thought he -- he had signed it --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Well, but --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- and on another occasion --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- but --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- he wasn't sure.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, I know, but this is your witness. This isn't their -- this isn't their witness. This is your witness.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And you're bound by what your witness says.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct, but I -- I don't think --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Then why aren't you bound by the things that he says in favor of the defendant, as well as the things that are said in favor of the Government? And if you are --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- if you are, why isn't this a statement that qualifies under Jencks? Because he said, "I think -- I think he showed it to me. I think I read it. I think I signed it." Now, if --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: And he --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- if you wanted --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- and then after that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- to disprove that that was -- wasn't the fact, why couldn't you -- that that was the fact, why couldn't you have brought the agent there to say, "Well -- well, I didn't show it to him. He didn't sign it and I didn't read it to him."

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: For a very good reason, Your Honor.
