The court pointed out to counsel in chambers when they had this one and a half hour conference about this matter that -- in fact, it was suggested by, I think, Mr. Louison, that the Government should call the agent, perhaps, to clarify this and the court said, "No, I can't allow that because the agent's testimony would be inadmissible." You may call him and he's available and you can subpoena him or you can have the Assistant U.S. --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that in the record?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, it's in the transcript which we have filed with the Court.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: What page is that?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, the -- the trans --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, because we didn't know the -- the existence of this transcript until after the petition for certiorari had been granted here. On page 10 of our brief, we refer to this transcript in a footnote. The transcript itself or the -- the conference in chambers is referred to by the judge on page 211 of -- of this record, at the top of the page. He speaks of a one and a half hour conference that he had wherein he listened to arguments and we had a full discussion of the briefs and where I finally decided and made a conditional decision. Now, in this conference, I have the pages of the -- the typewritten transcript where this appears. On pages 1831 and 1832, the judge finally decided to follow this homely procedure of -- of showing the Interview Report to the witness which he apparently had done with other witnesses in this trial to see whether he -- he agreed with this and adopted it as a statement. And at that point, Mr. Louison said, "Your Honor, I think we should have Special Agent Toomey in here to explain where he got the document." The Court, "No, listen. Special Agent Toomey is a witness you have a right to subpoena and bring in here. Obviously -- let me tell you something, now, Mr. Louison, I'm glad you spoke of that and I'm glad Mr. O'Donnell has been over it. Obviously, no special agent, no peace officer can testify unless he can testify to something that is admissible. And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What -- what were they talking about? I don't understand why -- it isn't axiomatically to me that what Toomey could have testified wasn't admissible or not.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This is a right -- I don't know what the conflict.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Because Toomey could offer no substantive evidence in the case. He had apparently only talked to this witness.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: That's what the judge was talking about, Judge McCarthy.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's right and secondly --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: The question of whether he could have testified to the contents of the statement --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- is an entirely different matter.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's right and secondly, if -- if it's a question then of impeaching Staula, the Government can't bring in Toomey and put him on the stand unless it thinks that it must impeach the witness.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, he probably could have but apparently, it didn't occur to him.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I -- I think he could have but -- but I don't think that injects error into this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, but I want to know what this footnote is about. What were they talking about in chambers?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, the conference is lengthy.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I know. What was the subject matter? What was -- why did they adjourn to the judge's chambers?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: They adjourned because at the end of one trial day, Staula was on the stand and counsel then made their demand and the Government info -- said --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Demand for -- for what you had called us for a note.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose they agreed to call the non -- of the nonexistence or not available document notes and the document which is in existence a statement.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, the document which is in existence is what we call a report of interview.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright, report of interview. Just to -- I want to differentiate the two. What was being discussed in chambers, whether the report of interview should be -- should be produced?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I -- the discussion in chambers revolved around the original notes --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- made by the agent and also, around this question of then shouldn't this also be produced in that the notes are not available. If they're not available, perhaps, we should be entitled to -- to this document.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, I want to know why Agent Toomey was -- why Judge McCarthy said he couldn't be called.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, he --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As just -- Justice Brennan just indicates, he certainly could be called to testify whether the so-called notes were read and supplied to or rather signed by the petitioner, couldn't he?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No doubt about that.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- I think that Judge McCarthy indeed could have called him in camera --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, it couldn't have been --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- and examined him.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Were they talking about that?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what he told me.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Mr. Louison suggested that the Government call him and Judge McCarthy pointed out that the Government couldn't do that. It would probably be reversible error because to put the agent on the stand as a government witness --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You say that he didn't sign it.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no, but to put him on the stand and have him tell about what the witness told him at the interview may tend to support the witness. It may give the jury --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, but -- but this is -- this is a preliminary inquiry as whether certain documents should be produced. We're not before the jury at the moment.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: We're -- we are in the judge's chamber who's to decide whether a call for document should be heeded.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, and on that issue, I don't understand why Toomey wasn't avail -- or wasn't an admissible witness in that preliminary inquiry before Judge McCarthy.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think he would have been but I don't think he was offered or -- or --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's -- that's -- I can't tell from this footnote what -- what it's all about.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it was --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: He wasn't offered by the Government but counsel for the defendant insisted that he should be -- should be in there and the Government refused to -- to produce him, isn't that right?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't think the Government refused to. I think that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- that the judge said that "You may subpoena him as a witness." That was the language I -- I quote.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, in other words --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: You may subpoena Toomey so he --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- in other words, a -- they -- he said it's the obligation of the defense to bring him instead of the obligation of the prosecution. Isn't that right?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- because --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- because the Government put him --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you don't mean that, if you'd wanted to, you couldn't have brought him.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I think he was available. He did testify in this case --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, no, I don't mean physically.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- at the outset.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I don't mean physically but I mean if you wanted to establish in camera before the judge on this proceeding that the -- the notes had not been shown to the witness, that he had not been asked to approve them and that he had not signed them, you could have brought the agent there, could you not, and -- and had him testify? But you didn't want to do that.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I don't know what the explanation for that is. The Assistant U.S. Attorney may have felt that there was simply no need to do it here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What -- what --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, whatever reason it was, you didn't want to do it. Isn't that right?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I don't know his reasons.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I didn't ask the reason.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I said you didn't want to do it.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, no, I don't think he did.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, alright.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: One would have to read, at least I would have to read, what's repeated, what -- how this conference to which footnote on this page occurs, what it was about and what it is that Judge McCarthy had in mind when he said, "You couldn't call the agent to give evidence." Of course, you couldn't call him up to give evidence as to what, what's his name? It's all about said but -- but what we're concerned with here is the circumstances that brought the original writings of the agent into light and secondly, what kind of things they were, whether they were within the -- the Jencks Act or not. And assuming they were within the Jencks statute and they're not being forthcoming, whether you could then -- whether the defendant could then call for the report of the interview. It was a very different question. They can't be all mixed up that you could ask about it.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I -- I think that if Your Honor reads that transcript, you will see that the court was trying to apply the statute, struggling for an interpretation of it in applying it in this case and perhaps, it didn't occur to him to call Agent Toomey in. He felt that -- that absent a sufficient showing by the defense counsel, that there -- there was some kind of a statement that had been destroyed that -- that he need not act further. He was satisfied when the Government told him that he didn't have the statement in its possession and -- and he indicated that -- that it was up to them to call.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I think --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I -- I think, certainly, that would be a wise approach to take, perhaps, in -- in similar cases.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, perhaps, the judge could have acted differently than he did. What we are urging is that this was not reversible error because the defendants were given an opportunity to call to me. They knew his name. I think he was available because, in reading the entire transcript of the record, I think only one-ninth of it is before you. In reading the rest of it, I saw that Toomey appeared in the early part of the trial to identify some photographs, unless that is a different Toomey. Perhaps, counsel can correct me if I'm wrong there.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I misconceive this whole case and I may well have misconceived it. If I -- if it isn't a fact that it was conceded or assumed that what was originally recorded by the agent was no longer in existence, that on the basis of what is recorded, he, after, would make a report which was in existence.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And all the shooting is about -- all the shooting is about is the production of that report or striking the testimony for failure to produce it. Isn't that what the case is about?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, I -- I think it is, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: What --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well -- well --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) production of the summary (Inaudible) Now, qualifying (Inaudible) It was restricted in response to the motion that the defendants made to strike (Inaudible)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what I included in my statement.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And it's only secondarily that that's going to be done or they're not entirely, at least alternatively, to have been allowed to use the summary (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I -- I think that if counsel would have conceded in answer to one question, if the summary had been made available, it certainly would have altered the -- the complexion of the case here. Now, as I see, I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But one can assume --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- one can assume throughout that the original documentation is gone. Isn't that -- isn't that --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the underlying --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- assumption?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, it's a sound inference to draw.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very well, I understand.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: If the original notes as --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Whatever you call them.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- as they're called here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I call them whatever. They're gone. There's no controversy about they having been withheld but the controversy is the consequences of their non-availability.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And we do differ with petitioners' counsel in that we feel that no statement was ever shown to have been in existence adequately enough to lay the foundation for production here and that there was an avenue opened to them, namely calling the agent if they had any doubt about it. They have raised the issue here and then tried to impale the Government on the horns of a dilemma, whether to call the agent or not, possibly inject reversible error into the trial. And in this posture of the thing, we don't think the question has been, adequately enough, explored below to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I thought you didn't read what the case was about but -- but now, you -- you destroy my belief that was clear. You -- you suggest now that it wasn't sufficiently shown that the original document were not available. I thought you'd agree --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, I'm sorry, Your Honor. It's -- it's well shown that they were non-available. I don't think it was adequately shown that these original notes or documents that were destroyed were ever the kind of thing that would be producible under -- under the statute. I think that's where we take grave issue with the petitioners.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that, if they were not producible, if they were not compellable under the Jencks statute, certainly, and then we wouldn't be compellable.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's -- that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, even if --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- they weren't producible, there's no dispute -- there was no dispute at the trial about the fact that they were no longer in existence, isn't it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct. There is no issue as to that, as I understand it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How is that established in the record?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Your Honor, the fact that they were destroyed?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That -- yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it wasn't affirmatively established in the record.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, how was it established?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, the Assistant U.S. Attorney told the court that he certainly didn't have in his possession any notes of this type and it's -- it's a common custom of the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, let's not talk about custom. You say it was clearly established in the record that -- that the notes were destroyed. I asked you how and where that was clearly established.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I don't think it is established.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, then, we're -- then, you don't need to go any further.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: It is possible that the agent could have wrongfully secreted the notes but that isn't shown either and we have no reason to suspect, as one member of this Court said, that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- federal officers go around looking for opportunities to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, no, I wasn't saying that.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- engage in (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I was just trying to explore your statement that it was clearly established that the notes were not in existence. That's all I want -- all I want to ask you about, where -- where it was in the record. You say it isn't there, so that's all there is to it.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: What we do have is the bottom of page 201 on the top of page 202 and that's about it, isn't it? That's all we have.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: What's it about?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's about all.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, Mr. Connor, we --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- plus --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- don't have any (Inaudible)

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Excuse me, I'm sorry.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Have you finished? I'm --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is there anything else besides the bottom of 201 and the top of 202 on this issue?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, except that in chambers --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is it about?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- that this conference in chambers, something -- something occurred on this.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And that isn't testimony, that's just a colloquy between the judge and -- and the Assistant District Attorney, isn't it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: The conference in chambers?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Who is Mr. Hurly?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Counsel for petitioner were there, too.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Who is Mr. Hurly?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Law clerk of the judge, they -- they informed me. Mr. Hurly was --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, the judge was in a colloquy there with his law clerk and [Laughter] that's -- that's the way we make the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Let me ask you this.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, the judge -- [Laughter]

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How did this --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- stated on the record that the conference was to be very informal and that everyone was to feel relaxed because they were in his chambers.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: When -- when did it develop that there was in existence this report that is in existence? How did that come up? How did that come?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: You -- that there was an Interview Report?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: The Assistant U.S. Attorney proffered it to the court. He said, "This is all I have."

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But where is it printed at?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Just turn to that in the record.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, it's at the end of the -- the record. It's at page 212. That is the -- the report.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Page 12?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: 212, down below. That -- that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: 212 at the bottom.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That is the -- that is the statement but where -- where -- how did it come into the record? I understood Justice Frankfurter asked you.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: My question is, how did the existence of that -- how did the existence of that disclosed?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, when the -- when the court called on the Assistant U.S. Attorney to produce any documents he had, he gave them this.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is that page? I'd like to see it in the record.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: It's -- it's here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I think that answers the Chief Justice's question and mine. I don't think it ever came out explicitly that the original so-called note was not available, except he clearly hasn't got them but he produces this. And that's why I say the presupposition is the assumption of everybody was that they weren't in existence.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Your -- Your Honor, on page 201, at the bottom, the court says "Have you got in your possession any statement that was copied by an FBI agent which, in any way, would reflect a statement that this witness made? Which would in any way," he says. And Mr. Hobley, his clerk, says "No, Your Honor, we don't."

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he's quite wrong.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: He is the clerk to the Assistant U.S. Attorney, isn't he? He -- he is part of the U.S. Attorney's staff.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What? The law clerk for the judge?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, they told me law clerk and I misunderstood them to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He's an Assistant -- he's an Assistant US Attorney of a lower rank.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Excuse me, Hobley and Hurly are two different people.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: So --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Who's the clerk, Hobley or Hurly?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Hurly.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Hurly.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Hurly.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but Mr. Connor, this must have -- something must have been said about this before that, wasn't it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think there was at an earlier time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: "You see, Mr. Hurly," and he said he was a -- so that, it was -- if that is the transcript on page 201, Mr. Hurly, "It isn't. It is a report for an FBI agent which is a summary of the result of the interview. It is his interpretation of what happened." In other words, that's the second document.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct. This -- this is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That would be the present controversy that's involved. They wanted -- no, no.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They wanted this produced or the evidence stricken.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct. They say that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now -- but -- but there must have been -- wasn't there earlier a number that it was said to the judge that "No, we can't produce that but we can produce this," wasn't there anything like that?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think, at some point back, they were told that they had --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what I thought.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- they had a report that bore on it but they had no statement producible within the statute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that is why I said, for me, throughout this proceeding, there was an assumption by everybody that the original documentation by Toomey was not available but they then offered this subsequent reporting, isn't that right?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct. The -- the Government gave that to the court and the court looked at it and saw the nature of it and then, after this conference in chambers which went on for a long time, he went back into the courtroom and gave this to the witness to see whether he would adopt it as a state, which the witness did not do.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Then what happened?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Then, as far as the court was concerned, that concluded it, that is, the witness wouldn't adopt it, so it certainly was not producible in -- in the way that the court saw the situation and the case proceeded.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, where do you find the linking of Staula (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I don't find anything directly on that that Staula said --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) you might need any purpose that he made or the court made, copy of the statement?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, I think they were talking about something that the FBI agent wrote down.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, what page is that? Four. (Inaudible) Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: He's the District Attorney.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: He is another -- he is another Assistant U.S. Attorney.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, he and Hobley were trying the case.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: He was law clerk to the judge.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, that's beforehand because the United States had possession (Inaudible) says "I do not have them in my possession. I do not know that they ever existed." The court, "Then you have to make Special Agent Toomey," (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) find out for tomorrow morning or I could try (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes. Well, in this transcript, I do recall that -- that the Assistant U.S. Attorney informs the judge in this hearing in chambers that he's checked with the FBI and that there are no such notes in existence.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And that's all (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's right. In fact, Your Honor, on page 204 where the asterisks appear near the top of the page, this is the end of the court day. And the judge stayed up until one in the morning, he said, looking for the Holy Grail, that is, the interpretation of the Jencks Act and the next day, this conference occurred in chambers. I believe it consumed a good part of the morning. It was after that that the court resumed trial and that's where Dominic Staula resumed testimony, as appears on this page. This gap is confusing otherwise but those asterisks really represent conference in chambers which is covered by that transcript which we have filed here. And in that transcript, somewhere, as my recollection, that the Assistant U.S. Attorney said that he checked with the FBI and that there were no such notes in existence. Now, exactly what --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say why they were no such note?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say why they were no such notes?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, I don't believe he did. I don't believe he went into it. The judge, in this conference in his chambers, spoke of knowing about the FBI practices and how they go about interviewing witnesses and he knew that they often took written statements --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- that were preserved and sometimes not.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- you're right about this as far as Staula (Inaudible) that he was talking about, as he said, he read back to him and he thought he signed it. He was never thought to (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I don't think he's very specific about it.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I don't think Staula uses the term "statement". He says that he told the agent his story and that they asked him some questions and they --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) Certainly, he didn't talk about notes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, I don't think he -- he talked about them writing down what he told them. Now, that could mean terse notes or --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) notes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I say that it's most probable that they were notes because the -- if the FBI had taken a lengthy statement, they either would have him signed this, if it were a complete statement or even if he hadn't signed it, they probably would have retained it.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) that statement or do you understand the FBI man?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: There's nothing in the record done?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, nothing in the record but what I think is -- is a reasonable conclusion from the established practices.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: So, what (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I -- well, for one thing, I've been in U.S. Attorney's office myself.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, and --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you're here.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I have talked to the FBI about their practices to -- to be sure that I -- that when I came here, I didn't make a mistake in -- in putting forth the Government's position and to see that I hadn't overlooked anything.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, is it customary for the FBI, after -- after their agents have -- have interviewed a witness and they had written extensively and have shown it to the witness and the witness has adopted it as his own and signed it to thereafter destroy that document just because they made a summary? Is that the practice --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- of the FBI?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, sir, anytime that a witness would sign a statement, it would always be retained.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you would --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is their established procedure.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) your own witness here then by saying that he did not sign it? He says "I think I signed it."

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: His --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's your own witness.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, but then he said, later, he wasn't sure. He was equivocal on this point. And the Government --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, is there any --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- the Government can't help it if the witness' memory is poor and thinks he signed a statement when he didn't. That is the -- the difficulty.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, but you do have the man who -- who should have a very accurate knowledge of it and you could produce him for this record if you had wanted to do it. You could have produced the agent who -- who had said, "I just took a few desultory notes and -- and I -- I said, generally speaking, is this what you told me? And -- and then, I went back and made a summary." He could have said that very easily and cleared up a lot of these matters if that was the fact but you didn't choose to do that or to use him for that purpose.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, the judge asked the Assistant U.S. Attorney to check if he would and inform the court whether any such things were in existence and apparently, he did and the court felt that this was enough.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That if -- if the defense wanted to explore it further, it had avenues opened to it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you told me, a few moments ago, if I remember it correctly, that all -- all the United States Attorney reported was that there were no such notes in existence and I ask you if he also reported that there -- that there had or had not been any at one time and then they were later destroyed, and you said there was nothing of that kind. Now, if you were pursuing it to find out why -- why wouldn't you pursue that when you talked to your agent? "Are there such notes in existence as far as you know?" "No. There are none." "Were there any? Did you -- did you take any? And if so, what did you do with it?" But that --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- that, apparently, was not brought to the attention of the Court and not made available to -- not made available to counsel, certainly.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, I think the court, in the course of this conference, showed that he -- he assumed that undoubtedly, something had been written down by the agent but he also didn't assume that there was anything improper in -- in it now being non-available.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Connor, I have started to look through this transcript. It begins on page 1790 and as far as I've gone --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: You -- Your Honor--

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- several times, the United States representative said explicitly, "I'm requested to produce a non-existing document." He said that two or three times, which isn't challenged or questioned or in doubt or -- or seen as the odium thrown upon it. But I can't tell -- what precedes 1790? I can't tell what documents they're talking about. The judge is a notoriously defused talker.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, and he even makes some patriotic lectures --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, I know.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- in the course of this transcript.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I can't tell what they were talking about.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How can I -- where is the rest of the transcript?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: The -- the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What -- this begins at 1790 and this is taking place in the absence of a jury. So, something must have happened which provokes this chamber proceeding.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, what happened is what --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, don't you tell me, I want to know where I can get the stenographic minutes of what happened.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it's set forth in 204 in the record where they stopped with this problem of Staula's statement and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, what they're talking about in these -- in these stenographic minutes are what we're talking about here, is that it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's correct, that's what we're (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But then it's --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- in this Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- as clear as a bell that everybody assumed. It was stated explicitly in plain unequivocal English that the documents, originally, whatever they -- their nature was, were not in existence.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Counsel can't stand by and allow such a statement made by U.S. Attorney and if they question it in their minds not to put their questions on their tongues. So, there wasn't any doubt about it that they were not in existence.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, that's what every -- that's what they all understood to be the case then.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What was not in existence? I --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: The -- if -- if --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Whatever was taken down by Toomey.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It seems --seems to me like --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- if they began in 1790 and continued until 8 -- 1960 and I can't tell what it's about. What -- what is this thing here on page 212?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Your Honor, on 212, we have the -- what we call the "Interview Report". Now, this is what the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, is that --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- FBI agent.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- admissible in your judgment?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, not under the -- it wasn't producible under the Jencks Act.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Because it -- it wasn't a written statement. It wasn't a statement of proof or adopted by the witness. He has specifically rejected it at the trial when it was shown to him.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He said that he didn't give this statement?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: He said, "This isn't the way I told it to him. It's been --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he say it wasn't--

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- turned around."

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- did he say it wasn't the truth?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, he didn't say it wasn't true. He said, "It isn't -- it isn't the way I told it to him." And --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did it --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- the point--

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- did it conflict with what he had said or agree with what he had said?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No. It -- it -- there are little ambiguities in this --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- summary by the agent --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I mean if --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- that don't accord --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- he -- this --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- to the way he testified.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- this statement says there were two individuals connected with the robbery. How many were --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- were there according to him?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: There were three.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And this is the statement of this witness that said there were two individuals and not three?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No. He mentions two individuals, one man in a chino pants and another man in a blue suit. And then later on, the agent here, in reporting, says "Mr. Staula stated that he did not observe a third man in the bank." Now, that was what the -- the dispute is partly about here. If the agent uses this term "observe", it is ambiguous. He could have meant that the witness saw a third man but didn't observe his physiogamy.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I don't want --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- I don't want to get his --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, no, but --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- anymore in there, but I do want to ask this question because I haven't put it at least all of them and I haven't found this out. Non-existent, let's get back to that. Do you mean non-existent because they did not make any statement --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- or do you mean non-existent because it has been destroyed?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: We mean non-existent in a sense that whatever it was that the agent wrote down was destroyed --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You do not mean --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- before --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- that no -- do you mean that no such statement was made as Staula testified to here where it was read back over to him?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: No, we don't contend that.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you admit that one was made at that time?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, we're -- we're saying that the agent wrote something down when he talked to Staula, that, we're sure of. And it might just be better to say that whatever this written matter was, it was destroyed.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Has been destroyed.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Whether it's a note or a statement or whatever.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes, by then it --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: I'm --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's what you mean by non-existence?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: And maybe that gets over the semantic problem a little bit to say that whatever it was the agent wrote down, he destroyed. Now --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Would you be willing to stand on --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- would you be willing to stand on the position taken by the Government in the brief because your brief is written without adding this additional point that you made that there never was a statement anyway?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He don't --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, no, no, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You probably misstated?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: If I misstated it, I'm sorry. I -- I meant to say that we didn't feel that, on the record here, there's been a showing that a statement producible under the Act had ever been destroyed. That if something was destroyed here --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: I understand that, but the only thing you argue in your brief is, number one, that since the original statement, notes, whatever you want to call it, were destroyed, therefore, you couldn't be required to produce this secondary thing as a substitute because the Agent -- because Staula has repudiated the secondary notes of the agent.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That's -- that's correct, which, of course, gets back to one of the basic purposes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: But now, you've been arguing, as I understand it, that apart from that, apart from that, that even though this statement had been -- the original statement had been in existence, it was a non-producible statement under the Act.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Well, we say that there's certainly no showing that -- that it was a producible statement and the avenue is to establish this were open to the defense and that they could've called the agent and found out from him what he took from --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: -- from the witness. And that the Government would have been in a difficult position to do this, at least in open court. Now, it's also been raised here that the judge could have done it in camera. He didn't do it. But that still leaves us with a problem of whether that is reversible error. The judge here selected one method of dealing with this problem and we don't think that it was erroneous.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Two things that are clear, two things about which they ought not to be a dispute in anybody's mind when we arrive here tonight is, one, there was an original writing by Toomey which was, when asked to be produced, non-existent.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And secondly, that there was a report by the agent based on those original -- on that original writing which is concededly not within the Jencks statute.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you -- then, the doubts that are raised is not the doubts, but you say that since the defendant was asked for the production of a document, it was incumbent upon them to come within the condition for the production laid down by the Jencks statute. And you say further that those -- that they have not made good that requirement of them to -- to have that document produced were it in existence --

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- which is essential before they come to the next point, namely since it isn't in existence, the next best thing should be given to them or at least, if not, the evidence of the witness' about and this controversy struggles should be stricken.

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's the -- that sums it all up, doesn't it?

turns.name: ROGER G. CONNOR
turns.text: That -- that essentially states our position, Your Honor. I see I only have a few minutes and I would like to briefly talk on another point that we haven't been able to develop and that is, this question may arise that if the witness rejected this Interview Report as being his statement, was it inaccurate? And was it so inaccurate that maybe it should have been made available? And our answer to that is no. In our brief, we have analyzed some of these discrepancies between the Interview Report and what Staula testified to on the stand and shown that they are not substantial and that the judge who saw this report and who heard the testimony of the witness didn't think they were substantial enough either that it should result in turning over this FBI report to the defense counsel. For a -- one of the points was how many men were in the bank according to Staula. He said on the stand there were three men. He told the agent -- well, at least according to the Interview Report, he didn't observe a third man in the bank. That could mean that there was a third man that he didn't see very well or that he didn't see anybody. And of course, this illustrates the equivocality that sometimes creeps into reports of an interview and why witnesses should not be confronted with them on the stand. This goes back to the congressional purpose behind the statute, fairness to the witness in the process of impeaching him at trial. I'd like to point out that no bad faith on the part of the Government has been demonstrated below and I don't believe was claimed in the briefs submitted here and that we do have the court and government counsel below trying to do what they thought was the right thing in complying with the statute. And that the procedures open to the defense were not completely used. If they had any doubts about this problem, they could have pursued it further but did not. And the record is, therefore, somewhat equivocal on this point. We submit that no injustice has been done in this case and a complete reading of the briefs will disclose that this is true. Thank you.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Louison.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Louison, before you start, would you mind just clearing up in my mind. There was some -- some little question as to whether you and your -- your counsel agreed on -- on the situation or disagreed. And I -- I would just like to pursue the one question that I asked you.Do you vouch for the fact that the Government did not do anything irregular or do you say that, for want of the information, you do not -- you do not contend that the Government did anything wrong?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: There -- there is a difference.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's precisely what I intended. I think I used the words to Mr. Justice Frankfurter. I do not suggest any wrongdoing and of course, that was based on the fact that I had no evidence of it and as you sated about the same way you just did now to Mr. O'Donnell was precisely, as I understood, what I had said.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you ever suggest anywhere, anywhere before this answer, not that you suggested now, did you ever suggest anywhere either, one, that there might have been wrongdoing or two, that this is a nice way for the Government to get out of the Jencks case? Did you ever suggest that anywhere? Could I find it in your brief in the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Yes, the second thing you stated, Your Honor, is precisely what I have suggested --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is my belief.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- even to that conference in lobby, and I can read it for you which is just what I wanted to read.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, I know, but it's my belief to that. But did you ever suggest anywhere, you never threw the slightest spec of dust on the wrongdoing in this case?

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Did I throw it? No, Your Honor, I did not throw it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: No. I had no evidence of it and I wasn't to suggest it, and I did not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Your point was that it was immaterial --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: -- want the FBI agent as my witness.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- isn't it? Your point was that it's immaterial.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: That's right, that there must be compliance with the statute or there's a vacuum. I do have my rights and I don't have my rights.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: And if I may, if -- if it please the Court, read at this time, Judge McCarthy did say "I have a great-- " page 1806 of their transcript, "I have a great deal of confidence in this Bureau. I know how this Bureau operates." From -- there's an old accent Latin maxim, "ex nihilo nihil fit" from nothing -- nothing comes.If the original notes were destroyed, how, in the name of God, could a defendant bring in and have available for him that which a witness will say, "That was what he read back to me and I adopted it as the statement he made." "How are you going to show it?" In addressing the U.S. Attorney, he said, "All you've got to do if that's the case and defeat this Act by making copies and tearing up the originals." So, that point which you suggested as point two in which we impress upon the Court, it would happen way back at the time of the trial and was suggested in the Court of Appeals and it's pressed here in this Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's a very different thing from what Mr. O'Donnell said at the bottom of this Court. Mr. O'Donnell --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: All I say --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- with regards to wrongdoing and you specifically disavowed it when I put it to you.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I don't wish to engage in quarrel with His Honor. All I say is that I have no evidence of wrongdoing and as the Chief Justice heard from what I said, it was what I intended to say and what I understood in my mind. I had no evidence of it and I was not about to suggest it because I do not consider it important to the outcome of this case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But your associate had suggested that --

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Therefore, I was not going to engage in quarrel with the Federal Bureau.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But your associate had suggested in words explicitly.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: He said that there was an inference. He did not -- as I understood, of course, I don't speak for him. All I state is what my position was, as -- as the Chief Justice heard it and interpreted it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Therefore, we ought to be slow in making charges against even government people, unless there's some (Inaudible).

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: [Laughs] That's alright. My time has expired, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, when the red light comes on.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You do have two or three minutes more, I think, if you wish to use it.

turns.name: MELVIN S. LOUISON
turns.text: Then I wish to say this, Your Honor please. As the various Justices and yourself has interpreted this matter, it comes down to the point where assuming, now, all the best in favor of the Government but taking the position, as Mr. Justice Frankfurter stated, that there was a statement in existence at one time which was producible. Now, if that be so, now take the evidence in favor of the Government that it was either lost, destroyed or misplaced for nothing but the very best of reasons on behalf of the Government. What happens to -- to the defendants who have the protection of the statute and therefore, the statute was passed to limit the right to discovery after Jencks, and what -- what do we have now? The Government now says, "Well, we don't have it in our possession." And by the way, the statute says "in the possession of the United States". It does not limit it to the prosecuting attorney or any branch of the Government "in the possession of the United States." What do we have? The statute was passed to limit our demands. Now, we come within the limitations and we're left hanging in the air. And -- and I say that is -- that cannot be the intent of the Congress and as I read the prior cases in -- in the Rosenberg, Palermo, Jencks and what inferences you can take from all of them in the various opinions in them, there must be a solution and there must be an alternative. There must be a sanction, as I stated in my original argument. If the requirements of the statute are not complied with, then what happens? Now, true, the secondary evidence may be given to us or it may be stricken but there must be something. It can't be left hanging. You -- Your Honors will notice, when the time comes, that -- in studying the record, that it does appear in the record that Judge McCarthy ordered production of the statement. He says, "I will order it produced." There's been a foundation here. And then, he never referred to it again. It was left in a state of a suspended animation. Why? Because we're left with the same vacuum we're left here -- with here this afternoon. There seems to be no solution, unless our position is adopted that the solution as we -- the evidence is either stricken as is required by the statute or in the alternative, we get the secondary evidence. But I submit in closing that there must be a solution. The law wasn't intended to leave a vacuum.
